citation_short: UK House of Commons Research Paper 95/41 (1995)
title: Russia's Chechen War Continues
geographic_scope: Chechnya, with specific Grozny battle references
strengths: Contemporaneous synthesis with explicit civilian wording and battle-specific Russian casualty figures; useful for both numerator and denominator anchors.
limitations: Derivative rather than a microdata registry; some numbers are reported from other outlets and unnamed observers.
notes: Best explicit accessible source for a civilian death claim tied to the Grozny battle, plus an early-March remaining-population estimate.

citation_short: Hansen & Seely, War and Humanitarian Action in Chechnya (1996)
title: War and Humanitarian Action in Chechnya
date_scope: 1994-1996, with specific discussion of the 1994-1995 Grozny battle
strengths: Strong denominator source: 1989 Grozny population, Slav out-migration before the war, and mid-January civilian stock remaining in Grozny. Also provides a conservative direct civilian death estimate.
limitations: Not a named-victim registry; some figures are themselves estimates from humanitarian observers.
notes: Key denominator source for Grozny's baseline population, prewar outflow, mid-January remaining civilians, and a conservative direct civilian death estimate.

title: Russia
geographic_scope: Chechnya, with a Grozny-specific mortality estimate
strengths: Captures the widely cited Kovalev study with an explicit error band and date window for Grozny.
limitations: The quoted sentence says people 'died' rather than explicitly stating civilian status or a direct/indirect split, so it is not clean enough to use alone in the core DR path.
notes: Used as an upper-bound contextual mortality anchor because it reports a Kovalev study with an error band for deaths in Grozny.

title: Russia's Chechen Wars 1994-2000: Lessons from Urban Combat
pages_or_sections: p. 38; Grozny chapter and map discussion
strengths: Clear operational description of the assault objectives and the 6,000-man force that actually entered the city on New Year's Eve; useful for battle geometry and lower-bound force assignment.
limitations: Chechen force estimates are explicitly very wide and operational rather than personnel-accounting data.
notes: Provides the clearest operational description of the New Year's assault force and a wide initial defender range.

title: The Caucasus Conflict and Russian Security: The Russian Armed Forces Confront Chechnya III. The Battle for Grozny, 1-26 January 1995
author_or_org: Timothy L. Thomas / Foreign Military Studies Office
strengths: Provides operation-start force totals, notes the ambiguity of the battle endpoint, and gives a Jan. 21 estimate of 3,500 Chechen militants still in Grozny.
limitations: Heavily reliant on Russian press and official reporting; not a casualty registry and not designed for epidemiological measurement.
notes: Used mainly for operation-start force totals, the Jan. 21 defender-presence estimate, and battle-endpoint ambiguity.

title: Breaking the Mold: Tanks in the Cities
author_or_org: Kendall D. Gott / Combat Studies Institute Press
strengths: Useful corroboration on the end date of the main battle and the official Russian battle casualty figure; includes map 23 for the Jan. 20-Mar. 13 phase.
limitations: A later military history synthesis, not a primary casualty dataset; population figure of nearly 490,000 is higher than other baseline sources and was treated as an outlier rather than the denominator baseline.
notes: Useful corroboration on the main battle endpoint and the official Russian battle casualty figure.
